item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Image is owned by Kriss, and has been removed, as the creator of the image does not permit usage of their images without contact first.

I have contacted the artist with permission from ProcyonLotor. Until they respond back to me, the image will stay removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely agree with you on the suicide line. Though I no-voted this because despite the questionable tone, it did gave me the creep as the story slowly built up through the logs and notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one really creeped me out, can't put my finger quite on why

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although this isn’t great, it’s still the best of the “things what let you see the creepy humanoids staring at you” I’ve read, as opposed to the 3D glasses or the shirt or the gas mask, probably because the humanoids are actually doing things and seem to have a purpose.
The idea is good, but the tone in the description yanked me out of the plot faster than [EXTRANEOUS JOKE EXPUNGED]. It’s a great idea, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone in this one really is kind of awful, which is a shame. There's potential in it, although it would need a fair bit of work.

Actually, I think the incident log is the best part.

Forensic evidence at the scene suggested suicide.

Everyone seems to be jumping on this line. Some think that it blatantly means 'He committed suicide' - face value reading. Some think it means the Foundation is incompetent - labeling it suicide when there's all the anomalous mirror-breaking. But there's a really well-done point in this one line of writing that it seems like people are missing.

The FORENSIC EVIDENCE suggests suicide. That doesn't mean it's what it is, because it's pretty easy to fake a lot of forensic evidence. The implication isn't just that the guys on the other side of the mirror can break the mirrors - it's implying they can either pass through, or affect our world FROM their side. THAT part is way more terrifying.

Also, seriously, there is zero way this thing is Safe. Ignore literally everything else in the article and there's one simple fact that makes it Euclid minimum - they do not have every instance in containment.

-1 overall, but it could be a very good one with some touching up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, people get that part.

There were, however, no mirrors in the attic, and all of the mirrors in the house were broken in an anomalous way. Also note that they never said when, in those three days he was missing, he died. Consider the possibility that it was in fact suicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, something about the end of this article just kind of gets me. I was already really creeped out over how more and more of the "yellow men" kept coming to watch, but I feel like everyone is missing something in the ending. In the room he commuted suicide in, the mirror was not broken by a blow or a weapon. What if he did not commit suicide? What if one of the "yellow men" came out of the mirror and killed him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of yellow people with jagged holes for eyes, nose and mouth observing me from the other side of a mirror creeps me up so damn much now, and that's thanks to you. Upvoted, but you should check your tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what would be fun?
Let's set up a room just like the one on the other side of the glass and have someone see how many SCP-1218-2 are on the other side. Then get that many researchers and just start copying them. Every time an SCP-1218-2 writes something, a researcher writes something. If one leaves, a researcher leaves. Just troll them. Then again, that would probably get us all fired.

OOC, now I have to wave hi at every mirror in case someone is watching. This is another great one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I quite like this. The idea's pretty creepy, and I think the development of the narrative as a whole is pretty well done.

But good grief, is the tone horrendous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A typo that I'm not sure what the correct wording is:

It appears that at light levels below 1 Lux the our test subjects start to become uneasy around SCP-1218-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked this one, but I feel like it could use a bit more work on the standard clinical tone. I'm uncertain if the original author is still open to re-writes for tone, so I'll just post my suggestions here.

Suggestion 1
Suggestion 1

Description: All instances of SCP-1218-1 are numbered as SCP-1218-1-XX in the order they were recovered. As of the most recent update of this document, we have SCP-1218-1-1 through SCP-1218-1-32 in storage, minus only SCP-1218-1-4 (see Testing Logs). Instances of SCP-1218-1 all appear to be normal wall-mounted mirrors, most frequently rectangular, with sizes between 0.2 and 9.5 square meters.

My issues with this are "we have […] in storage" (which feels too narrative and not detached enough compared to most SCP articles) and "minus only" (which reads a little informal). I also would rather see the description of SCP-1218-1 right up front, since, well, this is the opening to the description. I would change it to the below:

Description: Instances of SCP-1218-1 all appear to be normal wall-mounted mirrors, most frequently rectangular, with sizes between 0.2 and 9.5 square meters. All instances of SCP-1218-1 are numbered SCP-1218-1-XX in the order they were recovered. Currently, SCP-1218-1-1 through SCP-1218-1-32 are contained, with the exception of SCP-1218-1-4 (see Testing Logs).

Suggestion 2
Suggestion 2

The creatures, hereafter referred to as 'the yellow men', wear moth-eaten suits identified as those in style during the 19██'s. SCP-1218-2 appear aware of viewers in front of SCP-1218-1 and have been seen to not only follow the movements of test subjects, but to actively take notes in what seems to be a ████ brand notebook.

This paragraph started strong, but the last two sentences stray from the clinical tone. I've removed mention of "the yellow men" entirely, since despite what the reader is told, SCP-1218-2 are never again referred to as "the yellow men" in the article, and for a clinical document, they shouldn't have been referred to as such in the first (and only) place. I also changed the description of their suits, as "moth-eaten" seems too colloquial for official Foundation use (is there empirical evidence that actual moths ate the fabric of the suits?), as well a few minor word changes that I feel improve tone. See below for my result:

All known instances of SCP-1218-2 uniformly wear clothing exhibiting marked deterioration, identified as suits in style during the 19██'s. Instances of SCP-1218-2 appear aware of viewers in front of SCP-1218-1 and have been recorded as not only following the movements of test subjects, but actively taking notes in what appears to be a ████ brand notebook.

Please take these suggestions with a grain of salt. I've never written an SCP.

On another note, I love how the [REDACTED] in the incident log is juuuuuust enough to disguise the fact that the mirror fragments were broken "from the inside out". At least that's what I got out of it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The payoff is "The Foundation assumed suicide, but…", and that alone is kind of telling of the Foundation itself. What's the suicide rate for researchers, that a quick glance is enough for them to take it at face value?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. I go with the rule of thumb that unless it is really necessary, code names are not a good practice in SCP articles. It doesn't go well with the (current) Foundation tone.

But then again, that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I look at that image, looking for something in the mirror.

There's nothing there.

It freaks me out. o__o

I gotta read this whole, but I like this already.

Well, I read it now and… it's a bit underwhelming in the end. There's lots of build up, but the payoff is "driven to suicide". Eh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I like it a lot, but I agree that the term "Yellow Men" needs to be dropped. Its not clinical, the term is only used once, and its claimed that it will be used again, but isn't.

Otherwise this skip is severely underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really do like the idea but i think it would be nice to get more info on what happens when you break them

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. I actually really like it but, I thought the point of an SCP was to look at them as objects. Giving them "the yellow men" title sort of gives me a sense of knowing, whereas keeping it simply SCP 1218 keeps it clinical and that much more scary.

Also, I would love to see more test logs. I still have a lot of unanswered questions. (Can they talk? Have a D class personal try to talk to them. It's not very clear on how they move. Do they stalk? Do the stand and stare? Do they actually walk around and peer through the mirror like what is being done to them? Do they sit?) And maybe give a little more of a hint what it bringing more of them. Seems to just suddenly happen. First you’re getting more mirrors, and then more of the yellow people? Was that meant to imply that the more mirrors there are the more they can transfer from room to room?

Just a few thoughts. Still, Up-vote for concept. This was quite creepy to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mostly like this, but the term "the yellow men" should be dropped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i love it, this is a good instance of not knowing what he discovered about who they are.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
As the original writer, I realize that this article needs a little work. I was trying to inspire a nagging feeling of being watched, that any innocuous mirror might just be glass with a dark room on the other side from which god-knows-what was watching you as you stared at your reflection, as you brushed your teeth. The idea that if you walk past a mirror in the dark, you might feel like you caught a flicker of movement from a room on the other side. To be honest the strange people weren't the point of the article, just there to explain the feeling of being watched. And the and-then-it-killed someone was to emphasize that these weren't just viewing screens, that they were physically connected to these other spaces.

I don't have a lot of writing experience though, which is why the formatting and tone seems a bit off. I'd happily incorporate any suggestions or even entire rewrites that can help this better exemplify the theme I was going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of the people just looking at you, but as Roget said, the description of them is kind of cliche and the log seems to go on kind of long. I think the people in the mirror just looking and taking notes is the interesting part of the article, but the 'and it killed a guy' seems kind of tacked on at the end.

Overall, I like the idea but I just don't think it's being used as well as it could have been.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is being placed up for review. Please check it out, it could use more attention.

I found the horror angle of "messed up zombie people" to be kinda cliche, and the log uses a lot of words where far fewer would suffice. Overall, I feel like this idea could be done a lot better with a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Horrendously creepy, and I, for one, liked it whole-heartedly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the tone, and I feel like this could possibly be executed better, but the something about it made me upvote by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. When I was a kid, I always went to a little place that had one of those "haunted mirrors," and it always freaked me out as a kid. This just really strikes a good tone with me. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many clothes fashions can be identified by the decade in which they were popular, especially older fashions. Redacting just the specific year would only be necessary if it were a very recent style, which, by redacting the decade, we imply it isn't.
As for the driven to suicide thing; I'd hate that too, which is why the log subtly implies that that isn't exactly the case.

Also, this was my first written SCP and I was trying to exploit those same paranoias you list. If you can think of other minor changes that might play up the creep factor a bit more, lemme know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, man… at first this was in creepy territory (I have a fear of empty public bathrooms and mirrors in dark rooms— mundane paranoias I love to see exploited) but the first person narratives of the tests added way too many meh details (why on EARTH would the decade of the suits be redacted?), and too many details is the death of the creep factor. The "driven to suicide thing" is what got my downvote; I think with work this could be interesting, as is it's just too vanilla.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's mostly due to my inexperience writing SCPs and is a skill I really need to build. If you have examples or suggestions, I'd love to fix it. I also welcome anyone who would like to edit directly to fix tonal issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The light source thing can work, if the light source is boxed in, so the light from it can only go through the mirror (or be absorbed by the box), and therefore doesn't brighten up the near side directly.

I don't think it's a test that needs to be added however, because it might take away from the creepy factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna upvote this one for potential, yes it's "mirror what links to other worldly nonsense" that's been done but it has a semi-unique spin on it. Like some others, it's mostly the non-clinical tone of some of the terminology that detracts for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one, really dings my creepy buttons. I think the suits take away from it, don't really add much.

Better than shadow people would be, i suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, but on the other hand they can be easily stored, have no hazardous effects or upkeep, and can be destroyed with little effort in an emergency…
…probably

I've adjusted the incident report slightly though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It might take a bit longer than I expected. I'm not going to have access to a pc for a while. My moms pc crapped out so I'm loaning her the one I was gonna use. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in with the "good idea, needs work" crowd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I thought it was that guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was somewhat better this time…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Forensic evidence at the scene suggested suicide

This troubles me too. There's nothing to suggest the researcher was contempating suicide, yet the Foundation seems to be taking this at face value. But they have gone back and made key redactions in the record, so clearly they know there is more going on here. You ought to drop some additional hints to either make his suicide either more reasonable, more suspicious, or at least more ambiguous. Right now it plays as a straightforward fact.

Finally, if any mirror in the world might be one-way, this surely ain't a Safe phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the simplicity of what we're presented with, and upvoted as it is. Still, I think if you tighten up the tone and logs, this could go from good to great.

two instances of SCP-1218-1 that he must have somehow sneaked past security

I don't believe this kind of breach is likely at the Foundation, but I have no problem believing he either found more in the wild on his own, or that 1218-2 "turned on" the mirrors in his house once they knew he was on to them. I would expect the Foundation can easily make similar guesses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh nice. Thank you very much for the encouragement, and I would happily work on this with you. I'm cool with you editing this for tone and if you want to collaborate on an audio log just message me directly. This is not only my first SCP, but my first creepy-pasta or, heck, fiction writing at all. I care far more about finding the best way to creep people out than having all the credit for the work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted, thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the direction youre going here, and I'm going to give you a tenuous upvote for encouragement. I want to reiterate, I find this concept to be freaky as hell, and I paid a bit more attention to the mirrors I came across in my life last night. :)

That being said, I think it still needs a lot of work on tone and cohesion. I should have access to a PC (posting from my ipod) after Sunday, I could do a bit of polishing to show you what I mean then (posting it here, not in the main article) if you'd like.

As for the "recognizing individual watchers" concept, I brought it up because played right it could add to the freak factor: noticing that test subjects start being studied by "their" watcher - almost turning it from observation to a stalking.

Also, and I'd be willing to try my hand at this as well if you'd like, by audio log, I was thinking of an actual audio log - an embedded MP3 file or maybe even a YouTube video (You could show an oscilloscope or perhaps a still of a bathroom mirror with the glow of a lit cigarette barely visible. I'm not bad at doing stuff like that, so if you'd like that, I'm willing to help.

Keep up the good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea isn't bad, but this is not finished yet. Keep working on it. For one thing, the description of the beings is less clinical and more creepypasta-ish. Fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These are very good points. I think I'll elaborate with some additional test logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The light source thing I'll readily concede, it was something I read long ago somewhere. Although going by the description, it seems that it still might work, and "no result" would still tell the foundation something.

The laser microphone, done right, could be another method to scare the bejeezus out of us - maybe even an audio log? Even if there's no speech, the ambient audio in the viewing chamber could be incredibly creepy.

 I'd accept "low-light cameras revealed no additional information." as an answer for that test as well.

The Foundation is meticulous and has huge resources, visual observation is where they'd start, not stop.

In addition, the article suffers from a dearth of details, things the researchers would be able to tell even from visual observation:

What are they writing in? Notebooks? Bibles? Ledgers? Clipboards? [REDACTED]?

How big are they? If they're smoking a known brand of cigarette, they'd probably be able extrapolate from there.

Are they fat? Thin? Talk? Short?

Do they have distinguishing characteristics - can you tell one individual from another? Are their suits identical?

Are they watching the mirror like a window, or a TV screen? In other words, do they sit still and watch, or do they bend to see things from a different angle?

These are just off the top of my head. I really like the concept here, but the article needs work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of people on the other side of the mirror just staring at you. It definitely triggers my "paranoia about being watched" reflex. And this article is a good start, but it just doesn't quite get there for me. I'm going to not vote at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That SCP you remember WAS this one. Apparently it was deleted, reworked, and reposted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair:

The first simply isn't true. If a brightly-colored or reflective surface is right behind the half-silvered surface, it might work; otherwise, the light returning from the other side just isn't powerful enough to ovecome the light bouncing off the near side.

The second is something the author should consider, if only for the sounds (or lack of sounds) in the room beyond. (They haven't noted any mouth movement, after all.)

Image enhancement software has definite limits; as the available information is reduced, the interpolations get more and more approximate. Low-light cameras might work, but only under the same conditions as given in the article; they amplify all of the light.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but this could use some work.

The tests seem somewhat limited. First of all, I believe that you can defeat one way mirrors by placing an extremely bright light in contact with the glass. Google is failing me, but I remember reading somewhere that it basically lights up the room on the other side enough to see into the other area.

Second, this technology can be used to listen to sounds on the other side of a window by reading the vibrations of the glass. Has this been tried?

Third, what about low-light cameras and image enhancement software? Do they show up on camera?

Finally, on a stylistic note, and this should be taken with a huge chunk of salt, maybe have the test logs end and pick up with a personal diary?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might want to change the picture. There was an SCP on here not too long ago with this exact same image and I thought it was a rewrite at first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I was a little worried about that, but I figured that focusing on the idea that any large mirror could actually be one-way glass with strange beings watching from the other side was distinct enough from 093 to merit it's own story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it's not bad, per se, it's somewhat lacking, particularly when you consider that the mirrors that show other places idea has already been done, in the tough-to-beat form of SCP-093.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi everyone, this is my first-ever SCP. I was really excited to try my hand at this, but am entirely open to criticisms and suggested changes I can make to improve this. Also, I'm not 100% sure on the formatting, especially for the testing logs. If someone has layout improvement suggestions, I'd love to hear them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…a bunch of people that hang around in bathrooms and the like and stare at you.

I don't like the idea to begin with, and the uninspired execution, sloppy redaction, and tone errors seal the deal. Downvoted.
